quiz_questions.question_text: Light microscopy uses _____ to illuminate specimens.

options.text: Visible light (with glass lenses to magnify)

quiz_questions.question_text: The maximum useful magnification of a light microscope is approximately:
Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) produces:

options.text: 3D images of specimen surfaces (detailed external topography)

quiz_questions.question_text: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) produces:

options.text: 2D images of thin internal sections (revealing organelle ultrastructure)

quiz_questions.question_text: Fluorescence microscopy uses:

options.text: Fluorescent dyes or proteins (like GFP) that emit light when excited — allowing specific molecule tracking

quiz_questions.question_text: GFP (Green Fluorescent Protein) is used to:

options.text: Tag specific proteins so their location and movement can be tracked in living cells

quiz_questions.question_text: The difference between magnification and resolution is:

options.text: Magnification enlarges the image; resolution is the ability to distinguish two close objects as separate

quiz_questions.question_text: Phase contrast microscopy is useful for:

options.text: Viewing living cells without staining (enhances contrast of transparent specimens)

quiz_questions.question_text: Confocal microscopy improves images by:

options.text: Using a pinhole to eliminate out-of-focus light, creating optical sections for 3D reconstruction

quiz_questions.question_text: Cell staining (like Gram staining in microbiology) helps by:

options.text: Adding contrast to visualize structures and differentiate cell types (Gram+ vs. Gram−)

quiz_questions.question_text: Cryo-electron microscopy (cryo-EM) is revolutionary because it:

options.text: Preserves specimens in near-native state by rapid freezing, revealing molecular structures without crystallization

quiz_questions.question_text: The unit of measurement for most cells is:
Advances in microscopy have been essential to biology because:

options.text: They revealed cells, organelles, molecular structures, and dynamic processes invisible to the naked eye

quiz_questions.question_text: Fermentation occurs when _____ is NOT available.
The two main types of fermentation are:

options.text: Lactic acid fermentation and alcoholic (ethanol) fermentation

quiz_questions.question_text: Lactic acid fermentation occurs in:

options.text: Human muscle cells during intense exercise AND certain bacteria (yogurt, cheese production)

quiz_questions.question_text: Alcoholic fermentation produces:

options.text: Ethanol + CO₂ (used in brewing beer, making wine, and baking bread — CO₂ makes bread rise)

quiz_questions.question_text: Fermentation produces only _____ ATP per glucose (vs. ~36-38 for aerobic respiration).

options.text: Regenerate NAD⁺ so glycolysis can continue producing the 2 ATP anaerobically

options.text: Lactic acid accumulation (though current research suggests it's more complex — microtrauma, inflammation)

quiz_questions.question_text: Yeast is used in baking because:

options.text: Alcoholic fermentation produces CO₂ gas that causes dough to rise (ethanol evaporates during baking)

quiz_questions.question_text: In winemaking, yeast ferments grape sugars until:

options.text: Alcohol concentration reaches ~12-16% (toxic to yeast, stopping fermentation)

quiz_questions.question_text: Obligate anaerobes are organisms that:

options.text: Cannot survive in the presence of oxygen (it's toxic to them)

quiz_questions.question_text: Facultative anaerobes like yeast can:

options.text: Switch between aerobic respiration (when O₂ is present) and fermentation (when it's not)

quiz_questions.question_text: Industrial fermentation is used to produce:

options.text: Bread, beer, wine, yogurt, cheese, biofuels, antibiotics, and many other products

quiz_questions.question_text: Compared to aerobic respiration, fermentation is _____ efficient.

quiz_questions.question_text: Trophic levels represent:

quiz_questions.question_text: Mitosis produces _____ daughter cells that are _____ to the parent cell.
The correct order of mitosis phases is:
During prophase:

options.text: Chromatin condenses, nuclear envelope breaks down, spindle fibers form

quiz_questions.question_text: During metaphase, chromosomes:

options.text: Align at the cell's equator (metaphase plate)

quiz_questions.question_text: During anaphase, sister chromatids:

options.text: Are pulled apart to opposite poles by shortening spindle fibers

quiz_questions.question_text: During telophase:

options.text: Nuclear envelopes reform around each set of chromosomes, chromosomes decondense

quiz_questions.question_text: The mitotic spindle is made of:
Centromeres are important because they:

options.text: Hold sister chromatids together and are the attachment point for spindle fibers (kinetochore)

quiz_questions.question_text: Humans have _____ chromosomes after mitosis (same as parent cell).
Mitosis is essential for:

options.text: Growth, tissue repair, and maintenance of multicellular organisms

quiz_questions.question_text: Errors in mitosis can lead to:

options.text: Abnormal chromosome numbers, cell death, or potentially cancer

quiz_questions.question_text: Colchicine disrupts mitosis by:

options.text: Preventing spindle fiber formation (blocking microtubule assembly)

quiz_questions.question_text: A cell in G0 phase is:

options.text: Not dividing (may re-enter the cycle or remain permanently non-dividing, like neurons)

flashcards.definition: A type of cell division that produces four haploid gametes from one diploid cell.
Exchange of genetic material between homologous chromosomes during meiosis I.
Random orientation of homologous pairs during meiosis I increases genetic variation.
A cell with one set of chromosomes (gametes in humans: n = 23).
A cell with two sets of chromosomes (somatic cells in humans: 2n = 46).

quiz_questions.question_text: Meiosis produces _____ daughter cells that are _____ to each other.
Meiosis reduces chromosome number from _____ to _____.
Crossing over occurs during:

options.text: Prophase I of meiosis (homologous chromosomes exchange segments)

quiz_questions.question_text: Independent assortment means:

options.text: Chromosomes always segregate the same way
Homologous chromosome pairs orient randomly at metaphase I, creating diverse gamete combinations

quiz_questions.question_text: The combination of crossing over and independent assortment produces:

options.text: Enormous genetic variation (2²³ combinations from independent assortment alone in humans, plus crossing over)

quiz_questions.question_text: Homologous chromosomes are:

options.text: Matching pairs (one from each parent) with the same genes but potentially different alleles

quiz_questions.question_text: In meiosis I, _____ separate; in meiosis II, _____ separate.
Nondisjunction is:

options.text: Failure of chromosomes to separate properly, resulting in abnormal chromosome numbers (aneuploidy)

quiz_questions.question_text: Down syndrome (trisomy 21) results from:

options.text: Nondisjunction producing three copies of chromosome 21

quiz_questions.question_text: Gametes in humans have _____ chromosomes.
Fertilization restores the _____ number.

options.text: Diploid (23 from sperm + 23 from egg = 46 in zygote)

quiz_questions.question_text: Oogenesis produces _____ functional egg(s) from each primary oocyte.
Spermatogenesis produces _____ functional sperm from each primary spermatocyte.

summary_sections.content_html:

flashcards.definition: A gene that slows cell division or triggers apoptosis (e.g., p53).
A mutated gene that promotes uncontrolled cell growth.
Programmed cell death — a normal process to remove damaged or unneeded cells.
An abnormal mass that is non-cancerous and does not spread.
A cancerous mass that can invade surrounding tissue and metastasize.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the main focus of the cell cycle and cancer?

options.text: Understanding concepts in The Cell Cycle and Cancer

quiz_questions.explanation: The study of the cell cycle and cancer examines the underlying biological mechanisms and principles that govern this area. Understanding the cell cycle and cancer helps explain how living systems function and interact. The answer is 'Understanding concepts in The Cell Cycle and Cancer'. The other options — 'Building machines', 'Cooking food', and 'Predicting weather' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which field of science studies the cell cycle and cancer?
quiz_questions.explanation: The cell cycle includes interphase (G1, S, G2 — growth and DNA replication) and the mitotic phase (mitosis + cytokinesis). Checkpoints regulate progression to ensure accurate division. The answer is 'Biology'. The other options — 'Geology', 'Astronomy', and 'Mathematics' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why do biologists study the cell cycle and cancer?
quiz_questions.explanation: Biology is the study of life. Its branches include zoology (animals), botany (plants), ecology (ecosystems), microbiology (microorganisms), genetics (heredity), and many more. The answer is 'To understand living systems'. The other options — 'To build computers', 'To predict earthquakes', and 'To study stars' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is the cell cycle and cancer best described?
quiz_questions.explanation: The Cell Cycle And Cancer is a field of biology that explores how organisms develop, function, and maintain life processes through molecular, cellular, and organismal mechanisms. The answer is 'A biological process or concept'. The other options — 'A type of rock', 'A weather pattern', and 'A mathematical formula' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What evidence supports our understanding of the cell cycle and cancer?
quiz_questions.explanation: Our understanding of the cell cycle and cancer is supported by controlled experiments, molecular analysis, observational studies, and decades of peer-reviewed research. The answer is 'Scientific research and experiments'. The other options — 'Ancient myths', 'Personal opinions', and 'Random guesses' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: How does the cell cycle and cancer affect living organisms?
quiz_questions.explanation: The Cell Cycle And Cancer affects living organisms through changes in cellular processes, gene expression, development, and physiological responses at multiple levels of biological organization. The answer is 'Through biological processes'. The other options — 'Through magic', 'Not at all', and 'Only in space' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is true about the cell cycle and cancer?

options.text: It is studied using scientific methods

quiz_questions.explanation: The Cell Cycle And Cancer is studied through the scientific method, involving hypothesis testing, controlled experiments, and data analysis to understand biological principles. The answer is 'It is studied using scientific methods'. The other options — 'It cannot be observed', 'It is not real', and 'It only exists in theory' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: The cell cycle is regulated by:

options.text: Cyclins and cyclin-dependent kinases (CDKs) at checkpoints

quiz_questions.question_text: Cell cycle checkpoints ensure:

options.text: DNA is intact, replicated correctly, and everything is ready before proceeding to the next phase

quiz_questions.question_text: The G1 checkpoint checks for:

options.text: Cell size, nutrients, growth factors, and DNA integrity before entering S phase

quiz_questions.question_text: Proto-oncogenes normally:

options.text: Promote normal cell growth and division (when mutated they become oncogenes)

quiz_questions.question_text: Tumor suppressor genes normally:

options.text: Slow cell division or trigger apoptosis (e.g., p53, Rb — 'brakes' on the cell cycle)

quiz_questions.question_text: Cancer results from:

options.text: Accumulated mutations in proto-oncogenes AND tumor suppressors (multi-hit hypothesis — typically 3-7 mutations)

quiz_questions.question_text: p53 is called the 'guardian of the genome' because it:

options.text: Detects DNA damage and either halts the cycle for repair or triggers apoptosis (cell death)

quiz_questions.question_text: Apoptosis is:

options.text: Programmed cell death (controlled, orderly self-destruction — important for removing damaged cells)

quiz_questions.question_text: A benign tumor:

options.text: Remains localized and does not invade surrounding tissue (generally not life-threatening)

quiz_questions.question_text: A malignant tumor:

options.text: Invades surrounding tissues and can metastasize (spread to distant parts of the body via blood/lymph)

quiz_questions.question_text: Metastasis is:

options.text: The spread of cancer cells from the original site to other parts of the body

quiz_questions.question_text: Carcinogens are agents that:

options.text: Cause cancer by damaging DNA (UV radiation, tobacco smoke, certain chemicals, some viruses)

quiz_questions.question_text: Cancer treatments like chemotherapy target:

options.text: Rapidly dividing cells (cancer cells divide rapidly, but healthy dividing cells are also affected — causing side effects)

quiz_questions.question_text: Stem cells are unique because they can:

options.text: Self-renew AND differentiate into specialized cell types

quiz_questions.question_text: Totipotent stem cells can become:

options.text: ANY cell type including placenta (only in very early embryo — first few divisions)

quiz_questions.question_text: Pluripotent stem cells can become:

options.text: Almost any cell type in the body (but not placenta — embryonic stem cells)

quiz_questions.question_text: Adult (somatic) stem cells are:

options.text: Multipotent (can become several related cell types — e.g., bone marrow stem cells → blood cell types)

quiz_questions.question_text: Induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) are created by:

options.text: Reprogramming adult cells back to a stem cell-like state using specific factors (Yamanaka factors)

quiz_questions.question_text: Bone marrow transplants work because bone marrow contains:

options.text: Hematopoietic stem cells that can regenerate all blood cell types

quiz_questions.question_text: A major ethical concern about embryonic stem cells is:

options.text: They require destruction of human embryos to obtain (iPSCs may circumvent this issue)

quiz_questions.question_text: Regenerative medicine aims to:

options.text: Repair or replace damaged tissues and organs using stem cells, tissue engineering, and growth factors

quiz_questions.question_text: 3D bioprinting uses _____ to create tissue structures.

options.text: Living cells (bioink) layered into 3D scaffolds that mimic tissue architecture

quiz_questions.question_text: The HeLa cell line (from Henrietta Lacks) has been important because:

options.text: It was the first immortal human cell line, used in countless medical breakthroughs (polio vaccine, cancer research)

quiz_questions.question_text: Stem cell therapy potential includes treating:

options.text: Heart disease, diabetes, Parkinson's, spinal cord injuries, blindness, and many other conditions

quiz_questions.question_text: Cloning (like Dolly the sheep) involves:

options.text: Somatic cell nuclear transfer — placing a somatic cell nucleus into an enucleated egg

quiz_questions.question_text: The greatest challenge in stem cell therapy is:

options.text: Ensuring transplanted cells integrate properly, don't form tumors, and aren't rejected by the immune system
